The **1980 Summer Olympics** ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Летние Олимпийские игры 1980, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian "Romanization of Russian"): *Letniye Olimpiyskiye igry 1980*),[[b]](#cite_note-3) officially branded as **Moscow 1980** (Москва 1980, *Moskva 1980*), were an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held from 19 July to 3 August 1980 in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), [Russian SFSR](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic"), Soviet Union.[[2]](#cite_note-sr-4)[[3]](#cite_note-IOC-5) These were the final Olympic Games under the [IOC](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") [Presidency](/wiki/President_of_the_International_Olympic_Committee "President of the International Olympic Committee") of [Michael Morris, 3rd Baron Killanin](/wiki/Michael_Morris,_3rd_Baron_Killanin "Michael Morris, 3rd Baron Killanin") before he was succeeded by [Juan Antonio Samaranch](/wiki/Juan_Antonio_Samaranch "Juan Antonio Samaranch") shortly afterward.[[4]](#cite_note-6)

Eighty nations were represented at the Moscow Games, the smallest number since [1956](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics"). Led by the United States, 66 countries [boycotted](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics_boycott "1980 Summer Olympics boycott") the games entirely, because of the [Soviet–Afghan War](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War"). Several [alternative events](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics_boycott#Alternative_events "1980 Summer Olympics boycott") were held outside of the Soviet Union. Some athletes from some of the boycotting countries (not included in the list of 66 countries that boycotted the games entirely) participated in the games under the [Olympic Flag](/wiki/Olympic_Flag "Olympic Flag").[[5]](#cite_note-Cousineau-7) The Soviet Union and most [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc") states later [boycotted the 1984 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics_boycott "1984 Summer Olympics boycott") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles").

The [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1980 Summer Olympics") won the most gold and overall medals at the 1980 Games, with the USSR and [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "East Germany at the 1980 Summer Olympics") winning 127 out of 203 available golds.

## Participation and boycott

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Participation and boycott")]

Main article: [1980 Summer Olympics boycott](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics_boycott "1980 Summer Olympics boycott")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Moscow_Olymp_vil_winter.JPG/330px-Moscow_Olymp_vil_winter.JPG)](/wiki/File:Moscow_Olymp_vil_winter.JPG)

[Olympic Village](/wiki/Olympic_Village_(Moscow) "Olympic Village (Moscow)") in February 2004

Eighty nations were represented at the Moscow Olympics, the smallest number since [1956](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics"). Of the eighty participating nations,[[9]](#cite_note-11) seven National Olympic Committees made their first appearance at these Games: [Angola](/wiki/Angola_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Angola at the 1980 Summer Olympics"), [Botswana](/wiki/Botswana_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Botswana at the 1980 Summer Olympics"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Cyprus at the 1980 Summer Olympics"), [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Jordan at the 1980 Summer Olympics"), [Laos](/wiki/Laos_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Laos at the 1980 Summer Olympics"), [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Mozambique at the 1980 Summer Olympics") and [Seychelles](/wiki/Seychelles_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Seychelles at the 1980 Summer Olympics").[[10]](#cite_note-12) It was also the first time [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Vietnam at the 1980 Summer Olympics") participated after the end of the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") and the Reunification of Vietnam. None of these nations won a medal.

29 countries boycotted the previous [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics") in protest against the IOC for not expelling New Zealand, which had sanctioned a rugby tour of [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") South Africa. The 1980 Summer Olympics were disrupted by another, even larger, boycott led by the United States in protest of the 1979 [Soviet–Afghan War](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War"). The Soviet invasion spurred President [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") to issue an ultimatum on 20 January 1980, which stated that the U.S. would boycott the Moscow Olympics if Soviet troops did not withdraw from Afghanistan within one month.[[11]](#cite_note-us_state_dept-13) 66 countries and regions invited did not participate in the 1980 Olympics. Many of these followed the United States' boycott initiative, while others[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] cited economic reasons for not participating.[[11]](#cite_note-us_state_dept-13)[[12]](#cite_note-Fail-14) [Iran](/wiki/Iran_at_the_Olympics "Iran at the Olympics"), under [Ayatollah Khomeini](/wiki/Ruhollah_Khomeini "Ruhollah Khomeini") hostile to both superpowers, boycotted when the [Islamic Conference](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation") condemned the invasion.[[13]](#cite_note-15) Neither the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") nor [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") (Republic of China) participated in the games, the former as a consequence of the [Sino-Soviet split](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split "Sino-Soviet split"). [Israel](/wiki/Israel_at_the_Olympics "Israel at the Olympics") also boycotted the Olympics, but also because of [Soviet anti-Jewish](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_the_Soviet_Union "Antisemitism in the Soviet Union") and [anti-Zionist](/wiki/Soviet_anti-Zionism "Soviet anti-Zionism") policies.

Many of the boycotting nations participated instead in the [Liberty Bell Classic](/wiki/Liberty_Bell_Classic "Liberty Bell Classic"), also known as the "Olympic Boycott Games", in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"). Those that competed had won 71 percent of all medals and gold medals at the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics") in Montreal. This was in part due to state-run doping programs that had been developed in the Eastern Bloc countries.[[14]](#cite_note-16)[[15]](#cite_note-17) As a form of protest against the Soviet intervention in Afghanistan, fifteen countries marched in the Opening Ceremony with the [Olympic Flag](/wiki/Olympic_Flag "Olympic Flag") instead of their national flags, and the Olympic Flag and [Olympic Hymn](/wiki/Olympic_Hymn "Olympic Hymn") were used at medal ceremonies when athletes from these countries won medals. Competitors from [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "New Zealand at the 1980 Summer Olympics"),[[16]](#cite_note-18) [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Portugal at the 1980 Summer Olympics"), and [Spain](/wiki/Spain_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Spain at the 1980 Summer Olympics") competed under the flags of their respective [National Olympic Committees](/wiki/National_Olympic_Committee "National Olympic Committee"). Some of these teams that marched under flags other than their national flags were depleted by boycotts by individual athletes, while some athletes did not participate in the march.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The boycott impacted the competitiveness of swimming, track and field, boxing, basketball, diving, field hockey and equestrian sports. Whilst competitors from 36 countries became Olympic medalists, the great majority of the medals were taken by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1980 Summer Olympics") and [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "East Germany at the 1980 Summer Olympics") in what was the most skewed medal tally since [1904](/wiki/1904_Summer_Olympics "1904 Summer Olympics").[[17]](#cite_note-19)

## Events, records and drug tests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Events, records and drug tests")]

There were 203 events – more than at any previous Olympics. 36 world records, 39 European records and 74 Olympic records were set at the games. In total, this was more records than were set at Montreal. New Olympic records were set 241 times over the course of the competitions and world records were beaten 97 times.

Though no athletes were caught doping at the 1980 Summer Olympics, it has been revealed that athletes had begun using [testosterone](/wiki/Testosterone_(medication) "Testosterone (medication)") and other drugs for which tests had not been yet developed. According to British journalist [Andrew Jennings](/wiki/Andrew_Jennings "Andrew Jennings"), a [KGB](/wiki/KGB "KGB") colonel stated that the agency's officers had posed as anti-doping authorities from the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) to undermine [doping tests](/wiki/Doping_test "Doping test") and that Soviet athletes were "rescued with [these] tremendous efforts".[[18]](#cite_note-20) A 1989 report by a committee of the [Australian Senate](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate") claimed that "there is hardly a medal winner at the Moscow Games, certainly not a gold medal winner...who is not on one sort of drug or another: usually several kinds. The Moscow Games might well have been called the Chemists' Games".[[19]](#cite_note-Hunt2007-21)

A member of the IOC Medical Commission, Manfred Donike, privately ran additional tests with a new technique for identifying abnormal levels of testosterone by measuring its ratio to [epitestosterone](/wiki/Epitestosterone "Epitestosterone") in [urine](/wiki/Urine "Urine"). Twenty percent of the specimens he tested, including those from sixteen gold medalists would have resulted in disciplinary proceedings had the tests been official.[[19]](#cite_note-Hunt2007-21) The results of Donike's unofficial tests later convinced the IOC to add his new technique to their testing protocols.[[20]](#cite_note-D._Derse2001-22) The first documented case of "[blood doping](/wiki/Blood_doping "Blood doping")" occurred at the 1980 Summer Olympics as a runner was transfused with two pints of blood before winning medals in the 5000 m and 10,000 m.[[21]](#cite_note-Sytkowski2006-23)

## Media and broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Media and broadcasting")]

Major broadcasters of the 1980 Games were [USSR State TV and Radio](/wiki/Gosteleradio "Gosteleradio") (1,370 accreditation cards), [Eurovision](/wiki/Eurovision_Network "Eurovision Network") (31 countries, 818 cards) and [Intervision](/wiki/International_Radio_and_Television_Organisation "International Radio and Television Organisation") (11 countries, 342 cards).[[22]](#cite_note-Brc-24) [TV Asahi](/wiki/TV_Asahi "TV Asahi") with 68 cards provided coverage for Japan, while [OTI](/wiki/Organizaci%C3%B3n_de_Televisi%C3%B3n_Iberoamericana "Organización de Televisión Iberoamericana"), representing Latin America, received 59 cards, and the [Seven Network](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network") provided coverage for Australia (48 cards).[[22]](#cite_note-Brc-24) [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), which had intended to be another major broadcaster, canceled its coverage in response to the U.S. boycott of the 1980 Games, and became a minor broadcaster with 56 accreditation cards,[[22]](#cite_note-Brc-24) although they did air highlights and recaps of the Games on a regular basis. [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") aired scenes of the opening ceremony during its *[Nightline](/wiki/Nightline_(U.S._news_program) "Nightline (U.S. news program)")* program, and promised highlights each night, but later announced that they could not air any highlights as NBC still had exclusive broadcast rights in the US. The [Canadian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation") (CBC) almost canceled their plans for coverage after Canada took part in the boycott, and was represented by nine cards.[[22]](#cite_note-Brc-24) The television center used 20 television channels, compared to 16 for the Montreal Games, 12 for the [Munich Games](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics"), and seven for the [Mexico City Games](/wiki/1968_Summer_Olympics "1968 Summer Olympics"). This was also the first time North Korea was watching, as KCTV (Korea Central Television) broadcast it as their first satellite program.

## Budget

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Budget")]

According to the Official Report, submitted to the [IOC](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") by the [NOC](/wiki/National_Olympic_Committee "National Olympic Committee") of the [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR"), total expenditures for the preparations for and staging of the 1980 Games were [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")1,350,000,000,[[24]](#cite_note-ioc1980-26) total revenues being [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")231,000,000.[[24]](#cite_note-ioc1980-26) To obtain additional funds for the competition, the Organizing Committee organized Olympic lotteries. The proceeds from the lotteries covered 25% of the cost of holding the competition.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

## Cost

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cost")]

*The Oxford Olympics Study* established the outturn cost of the Moscow 1980 Summer Olympics at US$6.3 billion in 2015 dollars.[[26]](#cite_note-28) This includes sports-related costs only, that is, (i) *operational costs* incurred by the organizing committee for the purpose of staging the Games, e.g., expenditures for technology, transportation, workforce, administration, security, catering, ceremonies, and medical services, and (ii) *direct capital costs* incurred by the host city and country or private investors to build, e.g., the competition venues, the [Olympic village](/wiki/Olympic_Village_(Moscow) "Olympic Village (Moscow)"), international broadcast center, and media and press center, which are required to host the Games. Indirect capital costs are *not* included, such as for road, rail, or airport infrastructure, or for hotel upgrades or other business investment incurred in preparation for the Games but not directly related to staging the Games. The cost for Moscow 1980 compares with costs of US$4.6 billion for Rio 2016 (projected), US$40–44 billion for Beijing 2008 and US$51 billion for Sochi 2014, the most expensive Olympics in history. Average cost for the Summer Games since 1960 is US$5.2 billion.

## Event highlights

### Archery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Archery")]

Main article: [Archery at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Archery_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Archery at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

* [Tomi Poikolainen](/wiki/Tomi_Poikolainen "Tomi Poikolainen") of Finland, who had not finished any of the previous three days' shooting higher than fourth, came from fourth on the last day to win the men's archery competition, scoring 2455 points. He won gold just three points ahead of a Soviet athlete.
* The women's archery gold was won by Ketevan Losaberidze (USSR), who was also the European, Soviet and world champion.
* The women's archery silver was won by Natalia Butuzova (USSR), who had set nine national records and three world records in 1979.
* The U.S. archery team was one of the strongest ever fielded, but due to the boycott, the team never had a chance to prove itself. This team held every record and featured 1976 Olympic champion Darrell O. Pace, who was averaging 100 points more than the winning score in Moscow at the time.

### Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Athletics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-W0801-0120%2C_Moskau%2C_XXII._Olympiade%2C_Marathon%2C_Cierpinski%2C_Chun_Son_Kon%2C.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-W0801-0120%2C_Moskau%2C_XXII._Olympiade%2C_Marathon%2C_Cierpinski%2C_Chun_Son_Kon%2C.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-W0801-0120,_Moskau,_XXII._Olympiade,_Marathon,_Cierpinski,_Chun_Son_Kon,.jpg)

Marathon in front of [Saint Basil's Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Basil%27s_Cathedral "Saint Basil's Cathedral"). The athlete 563 in the foreground is [Koh Chun-son](/wiki/Koh_Chun-son "Koh Chun-son") from North Korea

Main article: [Athletics at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Athletics at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

* Ethiopian [Miruts Yifter](/wiki/Miruts_Yifter "Miruts Yifter") won the 5,000 metres and 10,000 metres athletics double, emulating [Lasse Virén](/wiki/Lasse_Vir%C3%A9n "Lasse Virén")'s 1972 and 1976 performances.
* "I have a 90% chance of winning the 1,500 metres," wrote [Steve Ovett](/wiki/Steve_Ovett "Steve Ovett") in an article for one of Britain's Sunday papers just before the start of the Olympics. After he won the 800 metres Olympic gold, beating world-record holder [Sebastian Coe](/wiki/Sebastian_Coe "Sebastian Coe"), Ovett stated he would not only win the 1,500 metres race, but would beat the world record by as much as four seconds.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Ovett had won 45 straight 1,500 metres races since May 1977. In contrast, Coe had competed in only eight 1,500 metres races between 1976 and 1980. Coe won the race, holding off Ovett in the final lap, who finished third.
* Aided by the absence of American opposition, [Allan Wells](/wiki/Allan_Wells "Allan Wells") beat Cuban [Silvio Leonard](/wiki/Silvio_Leonard "Silvio Leonard") to become the first Briton since 1924 to win the Olympic 100 metres race.
* [Gerd Wessig](/wiki/Gerd_Wessig "Gerd Wessig"), who had made the East German team only two weeks before the Games, easily won the gold medal with a 2.36 metres (7 ft 9 in) high jump. This was 9 cm higher than he had ever jumped before.
* In the 1980 Olympic women's long jump competition, Soviet jumper [Tatiana Kolpakova](/wiki/Tatiana_Kolpakova "Tatiana Kolpakova") bested her compatriots and other competitors by setting a new Olympic record of 7.06 metres (23 ft 2 in).
* Poland's [Władysław Kozakiewicz](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Kozakiewicz "Władysław Kozakiewicz") won the pole vault with a jump of 5.78 metres (19 ft 0 in) – only the second pole vaulting world record to be established during an Olympics. The previous time had been at the [Antwerp Olympics 1920](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics").
* In the long jump competition, three women beat 23 feet (7.0 m) for the first time ever in one competition.
* [Waldemar Cierpinski](/wiki/Waldemar_Cierpinski "Waldemar Cierpinski") of the [German Democratic Republic](/wiki/German_Democratic_Republic "German Democratic Republic") ([East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany")) won his second consecutive [marathon](/wiki/Marathon_(sport) "Marathon (sport)") gold.
* [Bärbel Wöckel](/wiki/B%C3%A4rbel_W%C3%B6ckel "Bärbel Wöckel"), also of the GDR, winner of the 200 metres in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), became the first woman to retain the title.
* [Tatiana Kazankina](/wiki/Tatiana_Kazankina "Tatiana Kazankina") (USSR) retained the 1,500m title that she had won in Montreal.
* In the women's pentathlon, [Nadiya Tkachenko](/wiki/Nadiya_Tkachenko "Nadiya Tkachenko") (USSR) scored 5,083 points to become the first athlete to exceed 5,000 points in the event during Olympic competition, winning gold.
* For the first time in Olympic history, all eight male participants in the long jump final beat the mark of 8 metres (26 ft 3 in).
* [Lutz Dombrowski](/wiki/Lutz_Dombrowski "Lutz Dombrowski") (GDR) won the long jump gold. His was the longest jump recorded at sea level and he became only the second ever to jump further than 28 feet (8.5 m).
* In the triple jump final, [Viktor Saneyev](/wiki/Viktor_Saneyev "Viktor Saneyev") (USSR; present day-[Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)")), who won gold at Mexico, Munich and Montreal, won silver behind [Jaak Uudmäe](/wiki/Jaak_Uudm%C3%A4e "Jaak Uudmäe") (USSR; present day-[Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia")) and ahead of Brazil's world record holder João Carlos de Oliveira. Both de Oliveira and Australia's Ian Campbell produced long jumps, but they were declared fouls by the officials and not measured; in Campbell's case, his longest jump was ruled a "scrape foul", with his trailing leg touching the track during the jump. Campbell insisted that he had not scraped, and it was alleged the officials intentionally threw out his and de Oliveira's best jumps to favor the Soviets, similarly to a number of other events.[[27]](#cite_note-Siukonen-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-campbell-31)
* [Yuriy Sedykh](/wiki/Yuriy_Sedykh "Yuriy Sedykh") (USSR) won gold in the hammer throw event. Four of his six throws broke the world record of 80m. No hammer thrower in the world had ever achieved this before. As in Montreal, the USSR won gold, silver and bronze in this event.
* [Evelin Jahl](/wiki/Evelin_Jahl "Evelin Jahl") (GDR), the 1976 Olympic champion, won discus gold again. She won with a new Olympic record – 69.96 metres (229 ft 6 in) – having been undefeated since Montreal.
* Cuba's [María Caridad Colón](/wiki/Mar%C3%ADa_Caridad_Col%C3%B3n "María Caridad Colón") won the women's javelin, setting a new Olympic record.
* [Sara Simeoni](/wiki/Sara_Simeoni "Sara Simeoni") of Italy won the women's high jump, setting a new Olympic record. She had won a silver in the 1976 Games and would go on to win a silver in the 1984 Games.
* In track-and-field, six world records, eighteen Olympic records and nine best results of the year were registered.
* In women's track and field events, either a world or Olympic record was broken in almost every event.
* [Daley Thompson](/wiki/Daley_Thompson "Daley Thompson") of [Great Britain](/wiki/UK "UK") won the gold in the Decathlon. He won gold again at the Los Angeles Olympics.
* Soviet [Dainis Kula](/wiki/Dainis_Kula "Dainis Kula") won gold in the men's javelin. He also had the best sum total of throws, showing his consistency. He finished ahead of his teammate Alexander Makarov.
* Polish gold medallist [pole vaulter](/wiki/Pole_vault "Pole vault") [Władysław Kozakiewicz](/wiki/W%C5%82adys%C5%82aw_Kozakiewicz "Władysław Kozakiewicz") showed an obscene [bras d'honneur](/wiki/Bras_d%27honneur "Bras d'honneur") gesture in all four directions to the jeering Soviet public, causing an international scandal and almost losing his medal as a result. There were numerous incidents and accusations of Soviet officials using their authority to negate marks by opponents to the point that [IAAF](/wiki/IAAF "IAAF") officials found the need to look over the officials' shoulders to try to keep the events fair. There were also accusations of opening stadium gates to give Soviet athletes advantage, and causing other disturbances to opposing athletes.[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34)

### Basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Basketball")]

Main article: [Basketball at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

* Basketball was one of the hardest hit sports due to the boycott. Though replacements were found, five men's teams including the defending Olympic Champion United States withdrew from the competition in addition to the US Women's team.
* In the women's competition, the host Soviet Union won the competition beating Bulgaria for gold, Yugoslavia won bronze.
* The men's competition featured only the second instance of the US Men's Basketball team not winning gold with the first one being in [Munich](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics"). Yugoslavia took home the gold beating Italy in the final. The hosts, Soviet Union, winners in 1972, won the bronze.

### Boxing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Boxing")]

Main article: [Boxing at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Boxing_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Boxing at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

* [Teófilo Stevenson](/wiki/Te%C3%B3filo_Stevenson "Teófilo Stevenson") of Cuba became the first [boxer](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") to win three consecutive Olympic titles in heavyweight, and indeed the only boxer to win the same event in three Games. ([László Papp](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Papp "László Papp") from Hungary was the first boxer to win three titles). In boxing, Cuba won six gold, two silvers and two bronzes.
* The [Val Barker Trophy](/wiki/Val_Barker_Trophy "Val Barker Trophy") is presented by the AIBA to the competitor adjudged to be the best stylist at the Games. The winner was [Patrizio Oliva](/wiki/Patrizio_Oliva "Patrizio Oliva") of Italy, who won gold at light-welterweight.

* Apart from the boycotted Los Angeles Olympics, [Birgit Fischer](/wiki/Birgit_Fischer "Birgit Fischer") of [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany_at_the_Olympics "East Germany at the Olympics") won medals in each Olympics from 1980 to 2004. In the 500 metres kayak singles, she won gold in Moscow, silver in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), gold in [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona").

### Cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Cycling")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Krylatsky_Olympic_Velodrome.jpg/250px-Krylatsky_Olympic_Velodrome.jpg)](/wiki/File:Krylatsky_Olympic_Velodrome.jpg)

Olympic Velodrome in [Krylatskoye](/wiki/Krylatskoye_District "Krylatskoye District")

Main article: [Cycling at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Cycling_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Cycling at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

* Lothar Thoms of [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") won the 1,000-metre individual pursuit cycling gold, breaking the world record by nearly four seconds.
* The winner of the bronze in that race was Jamaica's [David Weller](/wiki/David_Weller "David Weller") who also broke the sixteen-year-old world record.
* In the 4,000-metre team pursuit qualifying heats, new world indoor records were set eight times.
* The 189-kilometer individual road race gold was won by [Sergei Sukhoruchenkov](/wiki/Sergei_Sukhoruchenkov "Sergei Sukhoruchenkov") ([USSR](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")).
* The cycling team road race was won by the Soviet team as they had done in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") and [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal").
* In cycling, world records were toppled 21 times.

### Diving

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Diving")]

Main article: [Diving at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Diving_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Diving at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

* As Aleksandr Portnov waited to do a 2 and 1/2 reverse somersault in the springboard final, cheers broke out in the adjoining swimming pool during the closing stages of [Vladimir Salnikov](/wiki/Vladimir_Salnikov "Vladimir Salnikov")'s world record breaking 1,500m swim. The diver delayed his start until the noise had subsided but, as he took his first steps along the board, even greater cheers broke out as Salnikov touched in under 15 minutes. Under the rules, Portnov, having started, could not stop before take-off. On protest to the Swedish referee G.Olander, he was allowed to repeat the dive, and went ahead again of Mexico's [Carlos Girón](/wiki/Carlos_Gir%C3%B3n "Carlos Girón"). Later protests by Mexico against the re-dive and by [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") that their [Falk Hoffmann](/wiki/Falk_Hoffmann "Falk Hoffmann") wanted to re-dive after allegedly being disturbed by photographic flashlights were both turned down by [FINA](/wiki/FINA "FINA"). FINA President Javier Ostas stated that the decision taken by the Swedish referee was the "correct one". FINA assessed all the Olympic diving events and considers the judging to have been objective. Portnov remained the winner, with Giron taking silver and Cagnatto of Italy bronze.
* Martina Jaschke ([East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany")) was fourth after the preliminary high dives, but came back to win gold on the second day of competition.
* Irina Kalinina ([USSR](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")) won gold in the springboard final. As a result of her ten dives in the preliminaries, she amassed a unique number of points: 478.86. In the previous four years, no diver had scored so many.
* In this final, the Mexican judge A. Marsikal allowed [Karin Guthke](/wiki/Karin_Guthke "Karin Guthke") ([East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany")) to re-take a dive.

Poland won the last of the 203 gold medals contested.
* The oldest medalist at the Moscow Olympics was [Petre Rosca](/wiki/Petre_Rosca "Petre Rosca") (Romania) in the dressage at 57 years 283 days.

* France took four gold medals in fencing.
* In the team sabre fencing final, for the fifth Olympics in a row, Italy and the USSR met. The USSR won as they did in Tokyo, Mexico and Montreal, while Italy's silver was its only medal in fencing.

### Gymnastics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Gymnastics")]

Main article: [Gymnastics at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Gymnastics_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Gymnastics at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

* Soviet gymnast [Alexander Dityatin](/wiki/Alexander_Dityatin "Alexander Dityatin") won a medal in each of the eight gymnastics events, including three titles. He was the first athlete to win eight medals at an Olympics. He scored several 10s, the first perfect scores in men's gymnastics since [1924](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics").
* [Nikolai Andrianov](/wiki/Nikolai_Andrianov "Nikolai Andrianov"), who had won gold on floor at both Munich and Montreal, was pipped this time by Roland Bruckner of East Germany. Andrianov retained the vault title he had won in Montreal.
* [Zoltán Magyar](/wiki/Zolt%C3%A1n_Magyar "Zoltán Magyar") (Hungary) retained the Olympic title on pommel horse that he had won in Montreal. He was also a three-time world champion and three-time European champion on this piece of apparatus.
* In the team competition, the USSR won the gold medal for the eighth consecutive time, continuing the "gold" series that started in 1952.
* In the women's gymnastics event finals, a Romanian gymnast medals on each piece of apparatus for the first time:
  + Balance Beam – [Nadia Comăneci](/wiki/Nadia_Com%C4%83neci "Nadia Comăneci") (gold)
  + Floor – [Nadia Comăneci](/wiki/Nadia_Com%C4%83neci "Nadia Comăneci") (gold)
  + Uneven Bar – [Emilia Eberle](/wiki/Emilia_Eberle "Emilia Eberle") (silver) & [Melita Ruhn](/wiki/Melita_Ruhn "Melita Ruhn") (bronze)
  + Vault – [Melita Ruhn](/wiki/Melita_Ruhn "Melita Ruhn") (bronze)
* Before the Los Angeles Olympics, the United States gymnastics federation proposed a change in the rules so that a head judge cannot interfere and meddle in the scoring of competitors.

The USSR men's handball team celebrating their victory over Yugoslavia

### Field hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Field hockey")]

Main article: [Field hockey at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

* Six countries competed in the women's field hockey: [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), India, [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"), [USSR](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), and [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe"). The gold medal was won by the team of Zimbabwe. Zimbabwe did not learn it would get a place in the tournament until 35 days before the Games began, and chose its team only the weekend before the opening ceremony. None of their players had prior playing experience on an artificial surface. Soviet Union won bronze.
* India won a record eighth title in men's field hockey. The Soviet Union won bronze.

* The USSR topped judo with five medals (two gold, one silver and two bronze). France came second (two gold, one silver and a bronze) and East Germany came third (one gold and four bronze). A traditional powerhouse Japan was absent due to the boycott.

* In the modern pentathlon, [George Horvath](/wiki/George_Horvath "George Horvath") (Sweden) recorded a perfect score in the pistol shoot. It had been achieved only once before in [1936](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics").

### Rowing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Rowing")]

Main article: [Rowing at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Rowing_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Rowing at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

* [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") dominated [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)"), winning eleven of the fourteen titles. The East German men won seven out of eight events, foiled from achieving a clean sweep by [Pertti Karppinen](/wiki/Pertti_Karppinen "Pertti Karppinen") of Finland (who defended his Olympic title from [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal")). East German women won four of their six events.
* In the rowing eights with coxswain, the British team won silver just 0.74 seconds behind East Germany.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sailing")]

* The sailing event was held in [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn"), [Soviet-occupied](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Baltic_states "Occupation of the Baltic states") [Estonia](/wiki/Estonian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Estonian Soviet Socialist Republic").
* Soviet sailor [Valentyn Mankin](/wiki/Valentyn_Mankin "Valentyn Mankin") won a gold medal in "Star" class. He won Olympic champion titles in "[Finn](/wiki/Finn_(dinghy) "Finn (dinghy)")" and "[Tempest](/wiki/Tempest_(keelboat) "Tempest (keelboat)")" classes before, and remains the only sailor in Olympic history to win gold medals in three different classes as of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit).

* The three-day skeet shooting marathon was won by [Hans Kjeld Rasmussen](/wiki/Hans_Kjeld_Rasmussen "Hans Kjeld Rasmussen") of Denmark.
* In the smallbore rifle, prone event, Hungarian [Károly Varga](/wiki/K%C3%A1roly_Varga "Károly Varga") captured the gold and equalled the world record.

### Swimming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Swimming")]

Main article: [Swimming at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Swimming at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-W0727-138%2C_Moskau%2C_Olympiade%2C_Siegerinnen_%C3%BCber_200_m_R%C3%BCcken.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-W0727-138%2C_Moskau%2C_Olympiade%2C_Siegerinnen_%C3%BCber_200_m_R%C3%BCcken.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-W0727-138,_Moskau,_Olympiade,_Siegerinnen_%C3%BCber_200_m_R%C3%BCcken.jpg)

[Rica Reinisch](/wiki/Rica_Reinisch "Rica Reinisch") with her gold medal in 200 m swimming.

* [Vladimir Salnikov](/wiki/Vladimir_Salnikov "Vladimir Salnikov") (USSR) won three gold medals in swimming. He became the first man in history to break the 15-minute barrier in the 1500 metre freestyle, swimming's equivalent of breaking the four-minute mile. He missed the 1984 Games because of the boycott but won gold again in this event at Seoul 1988.
* Salnikov also won gold in the 4 × 200 m relay and the 400m freestyle. In the 400m freestyle, he set a new Olympic record which was just eleven-hundredths of a second outside his own world record.
* In the Montreal final of the 400m freestyle, the seventh and eighth place finalists finished in over four minutes. In Moscow sixteen swimmers finished in under four minutes and eight of them did not make the final.
* [Duncan Goodhew](/wiki/Duncan_Goodhew "Duncan Goodhew") of Great Britain won the 100 metres breaststroke.
* Sweden's [Bengt Baron](/wiki/Bengt_Baron "Bengt Baron") won gold in the 100 meter backstroke.
* In the men's 4 × 100 metres medley relay, each of the eight teams taking part in the final broke its country's national record.
* The first Australian gold since 1972 came in the 4 × 100 men's medley relay,[[33]](#cite_note-35) with [Neil Brooks](/wiki/Neil_Brooks "Neil Brooks") swimming the final leg, the Australians swam the second-fastest time in history.
* East German women dominated the swimming events, winning nine of eleven individual titles, both the relays and setting 6 world records. They also won all three medals in six different races. In total they won 26 of the available 35 medals. As it was revealed later, their results were aided by the [state-sponsored doping system](/wiki/Doping_in_sport#East_Germany_(the_GDR) "Doping in sport").
* [Barbara Krause](/wiki/Barbara_Krause "Barbara Krause") (East Germany) became the first woman to go under 55 seconds for the 100 m freestyle.
* Backstroker [Rica Reinisch](/wiki/Rica_Reinisch "Rica Reinisch") (East Germany) was 20th in the world rankings for 100m in 1979 and not in the top 100 for the 200 m. At the Olympics she broke the world records in both distances winning golds.
* In the 100m butterfly, [Caren Metschuck](/wiki/Caren_Metschuck "Caren Metschuck") (East Germany) beats her more experienced teammate [Andrea Pollack](/wiki/Andrea_Pollack "Andrea Pollack") to win gold.
* [Petra Schneider](/wiki/Petra_Schneider "Petra Schneider") (East Germany) shaved three seconds off the world record in the 400m medley.
* As in Montreal, the Soviet women made a clean sweep of the medals in the 200m breaststroke. The title in this event was won by [Lina Kačiušytė](/wiki/Lina_Ka%C4%8Diu%C5%A1yt%C4%97 "Lina Kačiušytė").
* [Michelle Ford](/wiki/Michelle_Ford "Michelle Ford") (Australia) won the 800m freestyle more than four seconds ahead of her East German rivals.
* In swimming, 230 national, 22 Olympic and ten World records were set.
* The youngest male gold medallist of these Olympics was Hungarian backstroke swimmer [Sándor Wladár](/wiki/S%C3%A1ndor_Wlad%C3%A1r "Sándor Wladár") at 17 years old.

### Weightlifting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Weightlifting")]

Main article: [Weightlifting at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Weightlifting_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Weightlifting at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

* The standard of weightlifting was the highest in the history of the Olympics. There were eighteen senior world records, two junior world records, more than 100 Olympic records and 108 national records set.
* The oldest of weightlifting's Olympic records – the snatch in the lightweight class set in 1964 – was bettered thirteen times.
* [Yurik Vardanyan](/wiki/Yurik_Vardanyan "Yurik Vardanyan") (USSR) became the first middleweight to total more than 400 kg, he won gold.
* In the super heavyweight class, [Vasily Alexeyev](/wiki/Vasily_Alexeyev "Vasily Alexeyev") (USSR) Olympic champion at Munich and Montreal, eight-time world champion, who in his career set 80 world records, failed to medal.
* Soviet weightlifters won 5 golds.
* The new category in weightlifting – up to 100 kg – was won by [Ota Zaremba](/wiki/Ota_Zaremba "Ota Zaremba") of Czechoslovakia.

### Wrestling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Wrestling")]

Main article: [Wrestling at the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Wrestling_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Wrestling at the 1980 Summer Olympics")

* In Greco-Roman wrestling, [Ferenc Kocsis](/wiki/Ferenc_Kocsis "Ferenc Kocsis") of Hungary was declared the winner of the 163 pound class when the defending champion Anatoly Bykov was disqualified for passivity.
* Soviet wrestlers won 12 golds.

## Closing ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Closing ceremony")]

Main article: [1980 Summer Olympics closing ceremony](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics_closing_ceremony "1980 Summer Olympics closing ceremony")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/RIAN_archive_488322_Flag-bearers_of_states-participants_of_the_XXII_Summer_Olympic_Games_cropped.jpg/250px-RIAN_archive_488322_Flag-bearers_of_states-participants_of_the_XXII_Summer_Olympic_Games_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:RIAN_archive_488322_Flag-bearers_of_states-participants_of_the_XXII_Summer_Olympic_Games_cropped.jpg)

Misha, the mascot, formed in a mosaic as a tear runs down his face during the closing ceremony

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Russia_stamp_2000_%E2%84%96_572.jpg/250px-Russia_stamp_2000_%E2%84%96_572.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russia_stamp_2000_%E2%84%96_572.jpg)

Misha carried by balloons into the sky, commemorated by a 2000 postage stamp issued by Russia

Because of the U.S. boycott, changes were made to [the traditional elements of the closing ceremony](/wiki/Olympic_Games_ceremony#Closing "Olympic Games ceremony") that represent the handover to the host city of [the next Summer Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics") in Los Angeles. Among them, the [flag of the city of Los Angeles](/wiki/Flag_of_Los_Angeles "Flag of Los Angeles") instead of [the United States flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_States "Flag of the United States") was raised, and the [Olympic Anthem](/wiki/Olympic_Anthem "Olympic Anthem") instead of the [national anthem of the United States](/wiki/The_Star-Spangled_Banner "The Star-Spangled Banner") was played. There was also no "Antwerp Ceremony", where the ceremonial Olympic flag was transferred from the Mayor of Moscow to the Mayor of Los Angeles; instead the flag was kept by the Moscow city authorities until 1984. Furthermore, there was no next host city presentation.

Both the opening and closing ceremonies were shown in [Yuri Ozerov](/wiki/Yuri_Ozerov_(director) "Yuri Ozerov (director)")'s 1981 film *Oh, Sport – You Are The World!* (Russian: О спорт, ты – мир!).

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1980_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** [IOC records](/wiki/List_of_people_who_have_opened_the_Olympic_Games "List of people who have opened the Olympic Games") state Brezhnev opened the Moscow Games as "President", a title used at that time by the Chairman of the [Presidium of the Supreme Soviet](/wiki/Presidium_of_the_Supreme_Soviet_of_the_Soviet_Union "Presidium of the Supreme Soviet of the Soviet Union"), or *de jure* [head of state](/wiki/List_of_heads_of_state_of_the_Soviet_Union "List of heads of state of the Soviet Union"). (The office of [President of the Soviet Union](/wiki/President_of_the_Soviet_Union "President of the Soviet Union") was not created until 1990, a year before the nation [broke up](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union").) Though Brezhnev was *de facto* [ruler](/wiki/List_of_leaders_of_the_Soviet_Union "List of leaders of the Soviet Union") serving as the [General Secretary of the Communist Party](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union"), that title is not reflected in IOC records.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** Officially known as the **Games of the XXII Olympiad** ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Игры XXII Олимпиады, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian "Romanization of Russian"): *Igry XXII Olimpiady*)
